AltText: A stand of birch trees at the southern end of Skye, in the Hebrides. The trees are mostly bare, but a few autumnal leaves remain, blurred with a long exposure.
Title: Birch Tapestry
Description:
A stand of birch trees at the southern end of Skye. Birch is the textbook example of a pioneer tree. It thrives in barren, disrupted or damaged ecosystems and requires intense, direct sunlight to grow. Most trees require some shade when they are young. By colonizing bare ground, providing dappled shade and nutrient-rich soil from its decomposing leaves, birch creates the perfect conditions for late-succession species like oak. Eventually, the birch finds itself outcompeted for light, because although it grows faster, it cannot grow as tall as its competitors in its short lifespan.

Deprived of the full, direct sunlight they need, the birch trees eventually die out. But this is not a problem for the species as a whole. A single birch tree can produce several million seeds a year. Tiny and lightweight, with thin papery wings, they can travel for miles. The Sound of Sleat is just 400 metres wide at its narrowest point, so only a moderate breeze would be required to carry one of these seeds from the mainland. This would have been a necessary step for birch to become, as it did, the first pioneer woodland species after the last Ice Age, which reduced Skye to a barren and treeless tundra.
